message.content:
Here are three versions of an email about the *same situation*: a student needs an extension on a homework assignment.

---

### 1. TOO CASUAL

Subject: hw lol

hey mr. carter,

i totally spaced on the history homework that’s due tomorrow. i had a game last night and then just crashed. any chance i can turn it in late without losing points? i promise i’ll get it to you soon.

thx,
jake

---

### 2. TOO FORMAL / STIFF

Subject: Request for Extension on History Homework Assignment

Dear Mr. Carter,

I hope this message finds you well.

I am writing to respectfully request an extension on the history homework assignment that is due tomorrow, May 4th. Due to a school soccer game yesterday evening and subsequent exhaustion, I was regrettably unable to complete the assignment by the designated deadline.

I fully understand the importance of adhering to due dates, and I do not make this request lightly. If it would be at all possible, I would be most grateful if I could submit the assignment by Friday, May 6th, instead. I would, of course, accept any consequences you deem appropriate.

Thank you very much for your time and consideration.

Sincerely,  
Jacob Thompson  
Period 3 World History

---

### 3. POLITE BUT NATURAL (JUST RIGHT)

Subject: Request for one‑day extension on history homework

Hi Mr. Carter,

I’m writing to ask if I could have a one‑day extension on the history homework due tomorrow. We had an away soccer game last night and didn’t get back until late, and I wasn’t able to finish the assignment.

If it’s okay, I can turn it in on Friday instead. I understand if you can’t grant an extension, but I wanted to ask.

Thank you,  
Jake Thompson  
Period 3 World History

---

You can show students how the same message changes with:
- greeting (“hey” vs “Dear” vs “Hi”),
- word choice (“totally spaced” vs “regrettably unable” vs “wasn’t able”),
- sign‑off (“thx” vs “Sincerely” vs “Thank you”).
